content:
===BEGIN===
You might have heard that black holes are these mysterious cosmic monsters that swallow everything, but let’s break down what they really are. A black hole forms when a massive star runs out of fuel and collapses under its own gravity, compressing its mass into an incredibly small point called a singularity. Around this point is the event horizon—a boundary where gravity becomes so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape. This isn’t some exaggeration; it’s a straightforward consequence of Einstein’s theory of gravity. Think of it like a well so deep and steep that once you fall in past a certain edge, climbing back out is physically impossible. So, while black holes might sound like science fiction, their existence and properties are well-supported by decades of observation and physics. The "nothing escapes" part isn’t hype—it’s just how gravity works in these extreme conditions.
